Cult Classics · Off-Road Icons · Japan

The VehiCROSS:
America's Greatest Automotive Mistake

Isuzu built 5,958 of them. The world moved on. The ones who found one never let go. This is the story of the most overlooked, most capable, most impossibly cool truck nobody bought.

Category Car Culture
Read Time 10 min
Published May 2026

Picture this: 1999. The SUV market is exploding. Every American with a driveway and a car payment wants a big, boxy, truck-based beast to haul their family, their gear, and their sense of self. Ford Explorer. Chevy Tahoe. GMC Yukon. The formula was simple — make it big, make it tall, make it look capable. Capability optional.

And then Isuzu showed up with something that looked like it had been designed by a team of engineers who had never once been told what was popular, what sold, or what people expected. The VehiCROSS was not any of those things. It was small. It was wild. It was two doors only. It had titanium-colored fangs in the grille, a matte black hood, a spare tire locked inside the tailgate, and a 4WD system so intelligent it made everything else look like a horse and buggy. America looked at it, blinked, and went back to buying Tahoes. And that — right there — is one of the great automotive crimes of the modern era.

The VehiCROSS is having its moment now. Not because the world caught up to it. Because it never had to wait. It's been correct all along.

By the Numbers — Isuzu VehiCROSS (US Market 1999–2001)
Engine3.5L DOHC 24-valve V6 (6VE1)
Power215 hp / 230 lb-ft torque
0–60 mph~8.2 seconds
4WD SystemBorgWarner Torque on Demand (TOD)
TOD Sensors12 independent traction sensors
Ground Clearance8.5 inches
SuspensionMonotube shocks w/ external heat-expansion chambers
SeatsRecaro leather sport buckets
Wheels18" chrome (2000–01) / 16" polished (1999)
Total Production5,958 worldwide / 4,153 US
Original MSRP~$28,000–$31,000
Fuel Economy13 city / 17 highway

The Origin: A Concept Car That Refused to Stay a Concept

The VehiCROSS was originally unveiled as a concept vehicle at the 1993 Tokyo International Auto Show. The reaction was electric. People couldn't believe it was real. Its ultimate Japanese production release in 1997 was notable in that the production vehicle arrived with very few design changes and in a very short time — a feat accomplished through the use of inexpensive ceramic body-stamping dies and the reuse of readily available Isuzu parts. In an industry where concepts routinely spend a decade getting watered down into something forgettable, Isuzu essentially said: no, this is what we're building. Exactly this.

The design team, led by Shiro Nakamura and Satomi Murayama at Isuzu's European office in Brussels, was an international group including Simon Cox — at the time best known for designing the Lotus Elan's interior, but who would go on to design the Cadillac Cien and become head of design at Infiniti — along with Joji Yanaka, Andrew Hill, and Nick Robinson. The task was to build a "lightweight but tough, fun but environmentally friendly" SUV. What they delivered was something neither the industry nor consumers quite knew what to do with. It wasn't an SUV in the traditional sense. It wasn't a sports car. It wasn't a truck. It was all three, compressed into one of the most visually arresting shapes ever to wear a license plate.

The shortcut that made it possible is one of the great untold manufacturing stories of the 1990s. A conventional steel stamping die cost around $1.5 million and took four months to produce, and a typical car needed twenty to thirty of them. Isuzu instead used ceramic dies — a third to a half the cost, ready in about six weeks — and raided the corporate parts bin for the rest, borrowing the dashboard from the Rodeo and the entire chassis from the short-wheelbase, two-door Trooper RS. It worked brilliantly, with one catch: ceramic dies wear out fast. Isuzu calculated it could stamp roughly 2,400 VehiCROSSes a year before the tooling was gone — and then that would simply be the end of it. The scarcity was never a marketing decision. It was built into the molds.

There was a second shortcut, and it may be the most revealing detail of all. In the 1990s, every carmaker ran its designs past focus groups — show the clay to fifty members of the public, and if forty-nine frowned at the headlights, the headlights got softened. Isuzu skipped that process entirely. The VehiCROSS went from the designers’ screens more or less straight into production. Nobody sanded off the strangeness. That is precisely why, twenty-five years later, it still looks like nothing else on Earth.

The TOD: Twelve Sensors Before Anyone Else Had One

Here is where the story gets genuinely remarkable — and where the VehiCROSS separates itself from everything else wearing off-road badges in the late 1990s. Torque on Demand. Not a marketing phrase. Not a badge on a button. A genuinely revolutionary traction management system built by BorgWarner that made the VehiCROSS effectively unstoppable on any surface, in any condition, before the driver even knew there was a problem.

The US-only constant 4WD TOD system used 12 independent sensors to detect wheel spin and redirect power to the wheels with the most traction, giving the VehiCROSS a high level of traction on wet and icy roads. This wasn't selectable in the traditional sense — it was always thinking, always monitoring, always redistributing. TOD was way ahead of its time. In its simplest explanation, the Auto mode utilizes sensors that gauge slip from the rear wheels to talk to a computer and tell it to send power to the fronts and exactly how much. It's conventional today, but back then the system's ability to send a varying amount of power to slipping or gripping corners was quite complex.

While every other truck in 1999 was selling you a lever and a prayer, the VehiCROSS was running a closed-loop traction algorithm in real time. And then it also had a low-range transfer case for when you needed to get serious. The VehiCROSS combined a computer-controlled AWD system for on-road driving and a locked-differential low-gear 4WD system for off-road driving. Both ends of the capability spectrum, covered.

In Four Wheeler magazine's 2000 comparison test against the Tahoe Z71, GMC Yukon, Nissan Xterra, Ford Excursion, and Mitsubishi Montero Sport, the VehiCROSS scored highest of all six vehicles for Trail Performance, Highway Performance, and Mechanical Excellence.

— Four Wheeler Magazine, Four Wheeler of the Year 2000

The Suspension: Motorcycle Technology on a Street Truck

The truck was intended to showcase Isuzu's off-road technology, and is one of the few vehicles to ship with monotube shocks with external heat-expansion chambers — a technology normally reserved for off-road motorcycle racing. Let that sink in. The shocks on the VehiCROSS were borrowed from competitive off-road motorcycle racing. Not truck racing. Not desert racing. Motorcycle racing — where weight is everything and performance per pound is the only metric that matters.

The result was a ride that divided opinions but never bored anyone. The VX is notorious for its ultra-stiff ride, due in part to bump stops that were too long for their own good. On smooth pavement it could feel taut to the point of impatience. On dirt, gravel, rutted trails, and mountain roads, it felt like it was built for exactly that surface and nothing else. The suspension didn't absorb punishment so much as it converted it into grip.

The Record: It Didn’t Just Look Tough

Here is the part almost everyone misses. The VehiCROSS was not styled to look like an off-road racer. It actually was one. Isuzu built it to rally-homologation specification straight from the factory — the cladding, the short overhangs, the motorcycle-grade dampers were not costume, they were a rulebook. And then the company went out and proved it in the one arena that cannot be faked: the open desert, against the clock.

A VehiCROSS won its class on both Stage 2 and Stage 4 of the 1998 Paris–Granada–Dakar Rally — the most brutal motorsport event on the planet — and followed it with a class win at the 1999 Australian Safari. Let that reframe the entire car. The same vehicle Americans dismissed as a weird-looking mall crawler was, at that very moment, beating purpose-built machinery across two of the harshest landscapes on Earth. It earned its cladding.

The Design: From Another Planet, On Purpose

You cannot talk about the VehiCROSS without talking about how it looked. Because how it looked is still — twenty-five years later — genuinely unlike anything else on the road. Titanium-colored fangs in the grille. A matte black hood insert. Aggressive black plastic cladding wrapping the entire lower body. Short overhangs that made the truck look coiled, ready to leap. An 18-inch chrome wheel filling every inch of the wheel arch. The spare tire lived inside the tailgate, accessed from inside — a choice so distinct it became one of the truck's signature quirks.

"Too weird for this world, but it’s still a stellar off-roader."

— The Drive

Motor Trend featured the VehiCROSS on its May 1999 cover and included it in its Top 10 Sport Utilities for Most Unique Styling. It also won the 1999 Russian Spirit of Time Award from the Russian Design Confederation, presented at the Russian International Motor Show. Russia. The VehiCROSS won a design award in Russia. That is not something that happens to ordinary vehicles.

Inside, the leather Recaro racing seats and faux carbon fiber panels made up for any interior shortcomings. The dash was function over form — some called it plain — but those Recaro seats were genuinely exceptional. The kind of seat that holds you in place when the trail gets aggressive and still feels comfortable after four hours on the highway.

It was also, for 1999, genuinely loaded. Every VehiCROSS came standard with a limited-slip differential, skid plates, tow hooks, dual airbags, ABS, automatic climate control, a leather-wrapped wheel, keyless entry, and an in-dash six-disc CD changer. In its first US year you could have any color you liked, provided it was silver, white, or black; later years added Proton Yellow, a deep Emerald green, and red. And then there was the Ironman edition — not the superhero, the triathlon — with embroidered Recaros, unique badging, and a Yakima roof rack. Fewer than 400 were built. In a car this rare, that is a rarity inside a rarity.

The Designer: The Man Who Was Right Too Early

You cannot fully understand the VehiCROSS without understanding who drew it — because the people behind it were not provincial outsiders who got lucky. They were future giants of the industry, and the VehiCROSS was an early glimpse of exactly where car design was heading.

Simon Cox, the British designer most associated with its shape, came up through the Royal College of Art and Lotus — where he worked on the Esprit and the Elan — before establishing Isuzu’s UK design studio. The VehiCROSS was his breakout. What came afterward is the punchline: Cox went on to design the Cadillac Cien supercar, to shape the entire “Art & Science” era of Cadillac styling, to pen the Opel Speedster, and ultimately to become chief designer at Infiniti. Co-leading the project was Shiro Nakamura, who left soon after for Nissan, where he would go on to oversee the modern GT-R, the 350Z, and the Juke.

Read that roster again, and the VehiCROSS stops looking like a fluke. It was drawn by the very people who would define what the next twenty years of cars looked like. The vehicle was not wrong. The calendar was.

The Owners: They Said It. We Didn't Have To.

The VehiCROSS has one of the most passionate and loyal owner communities of any vehicle ever produced. These aren't casual car owners. These are people who found something rare and chose to protect it. Their words speak louder than any press release.

"My VehiCross has over 140,000 miles and I have driven it 102,000 of those over the last 4.5 years with no reliability issues and it is still running strong. This vehicle is the best in snow I have ever experienced and I live at 10,000 feet in the Colorado Rockies. It handles like a sports car, corners very well and climbs without hesitation. I think this is the most reliable vehicle I have owned. Isuzu engineering was excellent. It's too bad they are gone. I own three Isuzu SUVs currently."
VehiCROSS Owner · Colorado Rockies, USA · Edmunds
"How many people can say they have even heard of an Isuzu VehiCROSS? There has not been one day that I have had the car that someone doesn't ask about it. Someone even asked me if it was the new Hummer. Imagine part Jeep, part Land Rover, part sports car — and of course the wonderful Isuzu build quality. You can't miss with it. My friends love it and people are dying to get shotgun."
VehiCROSS Owner · CarSurvey.org
"The Vehicross is like a stubborn pig in mud out there — it is a pity to see it in captivity and out of production. I'm from Africa and worked as an expedition guide for several years. We travel Africa's worst roads year round — from Cape Town all the way up through the deserts and jungles. Isuzu together with Toyota are the KINGS for durability and reliability. Drove the VX 2,300 miles in 48 hours once — was a pleasure even on the blacktop."
Expedition Guide & VehiCROSS Owner · South Africa · EveryAuto
"The past three years spent in this little truck have been priceless, and the future is certain — I will never sell this little truck called the VehiCROSS. It is the most fun to drive of every SUV I tested, including Jeep. I say get one while you can."
VehiCROSS Owner · USA · Edmunds
"I've had my VX for about four years now, and it's honestly been such a great ride. I haven't had any expensive issues — the most severe thing I've had to replace was a pulley. The unique design gets lots of looks no matter where I drive and it's comfortable inside for any long drive. I've been through the Indiana winters with no problems. My biggest complaint is the lack of cup holders. But that's easily overlooked."
VehiCROSS Owner · Indiana, USA · Kelley Blue Book

The Competition: How It Stacked Up Then — and Now

In Four Wheeler's 2000 comparison, the VehiCROSS went up against the defining trucks of its era. What happened surprised everyone outside of Isuzu — and apparently some people inside it too.

Winner — Trail & Highway
Isuzu VehiCROSS
HP215
4WD SystemTOD (12 sensors)
SuspensionMoto-grade monotube
Weight3,924 lbs
SeatsRecaro Sport
Production5,958 total
Chevy Tahoe Z71
HP255
4WD SystemAutotrac
SuspensionCoil / torsion
Weight~5,050 lbs
SeatsCloth / leather
ProductionMass market
Nissan Xterra
HP170
4WD SystemPart-time 4WD
SuspensionCoil / leaf
Weight~3,760 lbs
SeatsStandard cloth
ProductionMass market

The VehiCROSS outscored all six competitors mechanically, for trail performance, and for highway performance in Four Wheeler's 2000 comparison. It finished runner-up overall only because the Tahoe Z71's sheer size gave it a cargo and passenger advantage.

Ahead of Its Time — and Wrong for Its Time

So if it was that good — and the magazines of the day agreed that it was — why did almost nobody buy it? Here is the answer, said plainly: the VehiCROSS was not a failure of engineering or vision. It was a failure of timing. In 1999, America wanted three rows, a tall roof, a familiar badge, and a monthly payment it could explain to the neighbors. Isuzu handed it a two-seat, rally-bred, computer-controlled 4×4 that looked like it had driven straight out of a video game — and priced it like a loaded Wrangler. The market did what markets do with things they have no category for. It blinked.

But look closer, and the timing reads very differently. The name itself — Vehi-CROSS — describes a vehicle built to cross between worlds, from the rock garden to the grocery run, a full decade before “crossover” became the best-selling shape on Earth. While Detroit chased the retro wave — the PT Cruiser, the reborn Thunderbird, nostalgia sold as the future — Isuzu went the exact opposite direction and built the actual future: small, aggressive, armored in cladding, all-wheel drive, as content in a city as on a mountain. Today every manufacturer sells some version of that car. The VehiCROSS got there first, and got laughed at for it. The FJ Cruiser that arrived years later borrowed nearly the whole formula — polarizing looks, awkward doors, serious capability on humble underpinnings — and was hailed as a stroke of genius.

That is what “ahead of its time” truly means. Not that the world was not ready. That the world had not caught up yet.

Jalopnik ran down the checklist — a beloved dead brand, genuine all-wheel-drive tech, true rarity, a shape that “still seems futuristic today” — and concluded the VehiCROSS is a future classic.
Jalopnik · Future Classic

Which is also why the VehiCROSS asks something of you that most cars do not: that you understand it before you judge it. It was never built to be a vehicle for everyone, and it never pretended otherwise. It is a diamond in the rough — a machine that demands the right owner, someone who can see past the plastic cladding and the near-useless rear window to the rally car, the design landmark, the rolling act of conviction underneath. Most people walked straight past it. They still do. And that, precisely, is the opportunity.

Reliability: The Isuzu Secret Nobody Talks About

Here is the part that surprises people most — especially people who assume that a rare, weird, complex Japanese SUV from the late 1990s must be a reliability nightmare. The 2001 Isuzu VehiCROSS receives an average consumer rating of 4.7 overall and 4.8 for reliability on Kelley Blue Book — and 97% of owners say they would recommend it. 97%. That is not a reliability number. That is a love letter.

Known issues exist, as they do with any vehicle of this age. Electrical gremlins can appear on high-mileage examples — fuses, window regulators, corroded connections. The automatic transmission can develop shifting quirks if fluid changes were skipped. Suspension bushings wear over time. But the engine? The TOD system? The body? Those are built the way Isuzu built everything — to outlast the roads they drove on.

One owner reported: "VehiCROSS is fun to drive and awesomely reliable. Of the three years I've owned it, total repair cost was only for a fuel pump." An expedition guide from South Africa ran one across the continent repeatedly. A Colorado owner at 10,000 feet drove 102,000 miles in 4.5 years without a single significant issue. These are not outliers. They are the pattern.

The Price: Then vs. Now

Here is where the VehiCROSS story becomes genuinely fascinating from an ownership perspective. When new, it was priced accessibly for what it offered. Today, the market has started to figure out what it's been sitting on — but it hasn't fully caught up yet. There is still a window.

Original MSRP (1999–2001)
$28,000–$31,000
~$52,000–$58,000 in 2026 dollars.
Full Recaro leather, Borwarner TOD,
motorcycle-grade suspension included.
Current Market (2026)
$3,500–$18,000
Clean, low-mile examples pushing $15–18k.
Average condition: $4,000–$8,000.
Collectors are starting to notice.

The calculus here is extraordinary. One Allroad owner noted that the car new, adjusted for inflation, would cost north of $65k — yet a used one could be had for under $10k. The same logic applies to the VehiCROSS with even more force: you are buying a 5,958-unit limited production run, race-grade suspension, intelligent 4WD technology, and Recaro seats — for what a used base-model compact crossover costs in 2026. The window on these prices is closing. Values on well-documented examples have been creeping upward steadily as the enthusiast community discovers what was hiding in plain sight.

The Downside: Because Every Legend Has One

Honesty demands it. The VehiCROSS was not perfect, and pretending otherwise would be a disservice to the people who love it most. The rear visibility was, by near-universal consensus, genuinely terrible. The rear window was practically useless and the C-pillars hid cars. Parking in a city required spatial awareness and a healthy relationship with your mirrors. The rear seat was accessible but not exactly welcoming — getting in the back was an act of commitment rather than convenience. The lack of cup holders was a genuine and recurring complaint. Fuel economy at 13 city / 17 highway was the price of constant 4WD and a 3.5-liter V6. Parts availability, while improved thanks to the passionate owner community and Isuzu Trooper crossover compatibility, can still require patience and research.

None of this changes what the VehiCROSS is. It just makes it honest.

The Cult: Only 5,958 Were Made. All of Them Matter.

Isuzu intentionally limited production to only 5,958 vehicles — the story goes that there was one set of tooling, and once it wore out, that was it. No more VX. Whether or not that legend is entirely accurate, it captures something real about the vehicle: its scarcity was baked in from the beginning. Isuzu built the VehiCROSS as a halo car — a statement of intent, a showcase of technology, a signal to the world about what the company could do when it stopped playing it safe.

The world, largely, did not listen at the time. But the community that formed around the VehiCROSS is one of the most dedicated in automotive culture. Forums still active. Parts tracked down globally. Restorations documented with the care of museum curators. Build threads that run to hundreds of pages. Owners who have had their trucks for fifteen, twenty years and show no signs of stopping. When Hollywood needed a believable car of the future for the 2000 film Mission to Mars — set, fittingly, in the year 2020 — it reached for the VehiCROSS: specifically the open-top VX-02 concept, driven by the film’s star Gary Sinise with jet-engine sound effects dubbed over its V6. We are now well past 2020, and the quiet joke is that the VehiCROSS still looks more futuristic than most of what is actually on the road. It was also selected by Austin Robot Technology as the platform for their robotic entry in the 2005–2007 DARPA Grand Challenge. A robot racing truck. Of course it was.

Isuzu itself seemed to sense it. In a press release promoting that very movie cameo, the company predicted the VehiCROSS would become “a prized collector vehicle in 2020 and beyond.” It was right — though it would not be around to enjoy being right, having quietly left the US market altogether by 2009. The two concepts that trailed the production car, the four-door VX-4 and the VX-02 roadster, were shown at the 2000 Los Angeles Auto Show, later donated to the Petersen Museum, and then — in one of the small tragedies of car history — destroyed by Isuzu for legal reasons in 2017. The production cars survived only because their owners flatly refused to let them go.

And here is the detail the collector market is finally waking up to, the one Hagerty keeps repeating: the VehiCROSS is rare, but its rarity was never because nobody wanted it. It was rare by design, capped by those ceramic dies. Values have stayed stubbornly affordable for what the car actually is — clean examples still trade in the five figures rather than the six — but the line is moving, steadily, in one direction. The people who already own them know exactly what they are holding.

"This vehicle is the best in snow I have ever experienced and I live at 10,000 feet in the Colorado Rockies. I think this is the most reliable vehicle I have ever owned. It's too bad they are gone."

— VehiCROSS Owner, Colorado Rockies · Edmunds

The VehiCROSS was ahead in every measurable way — technology, capability, design, efficiency of purpose. It just had the misfortune of being ahead in an era that hadn't yet learned to appreciate what ahead meant. Today, when the enthusiast community talks about the great overlooked trucks, the great lost legends, the great what-ifs of automotive history — the VehiCROSS is always on that list. Usually near the top.

Fewer than 6,000 exist in the world. They are not making more. The people who own them already know what they have. The question is whether the rest of us are paying attention.

The Carveyance Verdict
The Most Capable Truck Nobody Bought — and the Best Deal Left in Automotive History

The Isuzu VehiCROSS is rare, capable, visually unforgettable, and shockingly affordable for what it is. Twelve traction sensors in 1999. Motorcycle-grade shocks. Recaro seats. A design award from Russia. A 97% owner recommendation rate after 25 years. And a production run so small that every surviving example is now a collector piece that hasn't been priced like one — yet. If you find a clean, documented VehiCROSS, you are not buying a used truck. You are buying one of the greatest automotive legends America largely forgot it had. Don't make that mistake twice.
